---
title: "New essay: answering the retrodiction charge"
description: >-
  A new essay takes the retrodiction objection head-on: the A=1 lattice
  has no free parameters, its full specification is the figure on every
  post, and that figure was committed weeks before the first paper and has
  never changed — a frozen design you can audit in git.
author: "Jack D. Menendez"
date: "2026-06-09"
categories: [essay, methodology, retrodiction, audit]
---

A new essay,
[*A frozen design, in plain sight*](../../essays/posts/2026-06-09-frozen-design-in-plain-sight/index.qmd),
answers an objection the program has now met twice: that the wall of
`PASS` rows — the Dirac equation, the hydrogen spectrum to four
significant figures, gravitational time dilation — is **retrodiction**,
the lattice shaped after the fact to reproduce known physics.

The essay separates two things that word conflates: the **pejorative**
sense (you *tuned* the model to the answer — fatal) and the **benign**
sense (you *reproduced* something already known — what every unification
must do). It then answers the fatal one completely:

- **No free parameters.** The lattice is a fixed minimal object — six
  basis vectors, $\mathcal{A}=1$, one tick rule — with no knob to turn but
  the Planck-scale lattice spacing. You cannot curve-fit a model with
  nothing to fit.
- **The design is the wallpaper.** Its complete specification — every
  vector, the conservation law, the chirality, the tick rule — is the
  figure that previews **every page of this site**. There is no hidden
  layer to adjust, because the whole architecture is on the thumbnail.
- **And it is older than the papers.** The figure's source
  (`figures/lattice.drawio`) was committed weeks before Paper I's first
  Zenodo deposit, and its six basis-vector values have never changed —
  `git log -S'(1,1,1)'` on the public repo returns a single commit.
  The freeze is dated in version control and in the immutable Zenodo DOIs.

The essay is just as explicit about what this does *not* settle:
reproducing known physics from a frozen design is a *unification*, not yet
a *novel* prediction. The novelty lives in the
[claim map](../../papers/claim-map.qmd)'s `STUB` rows — the optical-axis
birefringence, the quantum Roche limit — the falsifiable bites the program
could lose on.

Pointers:

- Read it: [A frozen design, in plain sight](../../essays/posts/2026-06-09-frozen-design-in-plain-sight/index.qmd)
- The `PASS` wall and the `STUB` bites: [Claim map](../../papers/claim-map.qmd)
- The substrate: [Paper I — Geometry First](../../papers/paper-01-geometry-first.qmd)
